content:
# Exodus Chapter 31 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folio ~52r (PDF pp. 100–101); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 74–75 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_031_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_page_002.jpg`, Vaticanus: `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_031_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Calling of Bezalel — When God Fills an Artist with His Spirit (vv. 1–5)

**1** Then YHWH said to Moses: **2** "Look — I have chosen Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, from the tribe of Judah. **3** I have filled him with the Spirit of God, giving him extraordinary skill, understanding, and knowledge for every kind of craftsmanship — **4** to design artistic works in gold, silver, and bronze, **5** to cut and set gemstones, to carve wood — to create masterfully in every kind of craft."

---

### Oholiab and the Wise-Hearted — No One Builds God's House Alone (vv. 6–11)

**6** "And look — I have appointed Oholiab son of Ahisamach, from the tribe of Dan, to work alongside him. I have also placed skill in the hearts of all who are naturally gifted, so that they can make everything I have commanded you: **7** the tent of meeting, the ark for the covenant testimony and the atonement cover that rests on top of it, and all the furnishings of the tent — **8** the table and its utensils, the pure gold lampstand with all its equipment, and the incense altar, **9** the altar for burnt offerings with all its utensils, and the basin with its stand, **10** the woven garments — that is, the sacred garments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to wear when they serve as priests — **11** the anointing oil, and the fragrant incense for the holy place. They are to make everything exactly as I have commanded you."

---

### The Sabbath — A Sign Between God and His People Forever (vv. 12–17)

**12** Then YHWH said to Moses: **13** "Tell the Sons of Israel: 'Above all, you must keep my Sabbaths, because the Sabbath is a sign between me and you throughout your generations, so that you may know that I am YHWH, who makes you holy. **14** Keep the Sabbath, because it is holy to you. Anyone who treats it as common must be put to death. Whoever does any work on it — that person is to be cut off from among his people. **15** Work may be done for six days, but the seventh day is a Sabbath of complete rest, holy to YHWH. Anyone who does work on the Sabbath day must be put to death. **16** The Sons of Israel are to observe the Sabbath, celebrating it throughout their generations as a permanent covenant. **17** It is a sign between me and the Sons of Israel forever, because in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth, and on the seventh day he stopped and caught his breath.'"

---

### The Tablets — Written by the Finger of God (v. 18)

**18** When he had finished speaking with Moses on Mount Sinai, he gave him the two tablets of the testimony — stone tablets, inscribed by the finger of God.

---

## Meaning Notes

**vv. 1–5 — BEZALEL AND THE SPIRIT: THE FIRST PERSON FILLED WITH GOD'S SPIRIT (Heb. *va'amalle oto ruach Elohim bechokhmah uvitevunah uveda'at uvekhal-melakhah*, וָאֲמַלֵּ֥א אֹת֖וֹ ר֣וּחַ אֱלֹהִ֑ים בְּחׇכְמָ֛ה וּבִתְבוּנָ֥ה וּבְדַ֖עַת וּבְכׇל־מְלָאכָֽה):** The name *Betsal'el* (בְּצַלְאֵל) means "in the shadow/protection of God" — a name that announces his vocation before his calling is described. He is the first person in Scripture explicitly said to be "filled with the Spirit of God" (*ruach Elohim*, רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים). The threefold gifting — *chokhmah* (חׇכְמָה, "wisdom, skill"), *tevunah* (תְּבוּנָה, "understanding, discernment"), and *da'at* (דַּעַת, "knowledge, experiential knowing") — mirrors the attributes by which God created the world (Prov. 3:19–20). The artisan who builds God's dwelling is equipped with the same capacities God used to build the cosmos. The word *melakhah* (מְלָאכָה, "craftsmanship, skilled work") is the same word used for God's creative work in Genesis 2:2 — the work from which God rested on the seventh day. The connection between artistic work and divine creation is deliberate: Bezalel's craftsmanship is a human echo of God's own creative activity. The LXX renders the filling with *pneuma theion* (πνεῦμα θεῖον, "divine spirit") and the skills as *sophia* (σοφία), *sunesis* (σύνεσις), and *episteme* (ἐπιστήμη) — a slightly more intellectual register than the Hebrew, which carries a stronger sense of embodied skill and artisanal mastery.

The verb *qara'ti veshm* (קָרָ֣אתִי בְשֵׁ֔ם, "I have called by name") in verse 2 is significant: God does not merely assign Bezalel to a task — God calls him *by name*, singling him out with the same personal knowledge with which God calls prophets (cf. Isa. 45:3–4). The genealogy — *ben-Uri ben-Chur* (בֶּן־אוּרִ֖י בֶּן־ח֑וּר) — traces him through the tribe of Judah, the royal tribe. The first Spirit-filled person in the Bible is not a king, a prophet, or a priest. He is an artist.

**vv. 6–11 — OHOLIAB AND THE WISE-HEARTED: COMMUNAL ARTISTRY (Heb. *uvlev kol-chakham-lev natatti chokhmah*, וּבְלֵ֗ב כׇּל־חֲכַם־לֵב֙ נָתַ֣תִּי חׇכְמָ֔ה):** Oholiab (*'Oholiav*, אׇהֳלִיאָ֞ב, "my father's tent" or "the father is my tent") is from the tribe of Dan — the smallest and least prominent tribe. God pairs Judah's artisan with Dan's artisan, the greatest tribe with the least, ensuring that no tribe can claim exclusive ownership of the sacred craftsmanship. The phrase *kol-chakham-lev* (כׇל־חֲכַם־לֵב, "every wise-hearted one") uses *lev* (לֵב, "heart") as the seat of skill and intelligence — not mere emotion but the center of a person's capacity for thoughtful, purposeful action. Wisdom, in Hebrew, is not abstract knowledge; it is the ability to do something well. The LXX renders this as *panti sunetoi te dianoia* (παντὶ συνετῷ τῇ διανοίᾳ, "every one who is intelligent in mind"). The catalog of items to be built (vv. 7–11) is a compressed summary of everything commanded in Exodus 25–30, confirming that the artisans' mandate covers the entire tabernacle project. The *bigdei hasserad* (בִּגְדֵ֥י הַשְּׂרָ֖ד, "woven garments" or "service garments") in verse 10 is a term whose exact meaning is debated; it may refer to the colored yarns used in weaving, or to a specific class of garments for liturgical service distinct from Aaron's "holy garments" (*bigdei haqqodesh*, בִּגְדֵ֣י הַקֹּ֔דֶשׁ) mentioned immediately after.

**vv. 12–17 — THE SABBATH AS SIGN AND COVENANT (Heb. *akh et-shabbtotai tishmoru ki ot hi beini uveneikhem ledoroteikhem lada'at ki ani YHWH meqaddishkhem*, אַ֥ךְ אֶת־שַׁבְּתֹתַ֖י תִּשְׁמֹ֑רוּ כִּ֣י א֣וֹת הִוא֩ בֵּינִ֨י וּבֵינֵיכֶ֜ם לְדֹרֹ֤תֵיכֶם֙ לָדַ֔עַת כִּ֛י אֲנִ֥י יְהוָ֖ה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶֽם):** The particle *akh* (אַךְ, "surely, only, above all") that opens verse 13 is a limiter and intensifier — the rabbis interpreted it as meaning "despite everything I have just commanded about building the tabernacle, *you must still keep the Sabbath*." The Sabbath takes precedence even over the construction of God's dwelling. This placement is theologically explosive: the command to rest interrupts the command to build, establishing that the rhythm of cessation is more fundamental than even the most sacred labor.

The Sabbath is called an *ot* (אוֹת, "sign") — the same word used for the rainbow after the flood (Gen. 9:12) and circumcision (Gen. 17:11). It is also called a *berit olam* (בְּרִ֥ית עוֹלָ֖ם, "permanent/everlasting covenant") in verse 16. The Sabbath is not merely a law to obey; it is a covenant marker, a sign that identifies Israel as YHWH's people just as surely as circumcision does. Its purpose is relational: *lada'at ki ani YHWH meqaddishkhem* (לָדַ֔עַת כִּ֛י אֲנִ֥י יְהוָ֖ה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶֽם, "to know that I am YHWH, who makes you holy"). The Sabbath produces knowledge — specifically, the knowledge that holiness comes from YHWH, not from human effort.

The penalty language is severe: *mechaleleiha mot yumat* (מְחַלְלֶ֖יהָ מ֣וֹת יוּמָ֑ת, "whoever profanes it shall certainly be put to death"). The verb *chalal* (חָלַל, "to profane, to treat as common") is the opposite of *qiddesh* (קִדֵּשׁ, "to make holy"). To work on the Sabbath is not merely to break a rule; it is to profane what God has sanctified, to treat the holy as though it were ordinary. The additional penalty *venikretah hannefesh hahi miqqerev ammeiha* (וְנִכְרְתָ֛ה הַנֶּ֥פֶשׁ הַהִ֖וא מִקֶּ֥רֶב עַמֶּֽיהָ, "that person shall be cut off from among his people") uses *karet* (כָּרֵת), the penalty of excision from the community — whether by divine action, exile, or loss of lineage is debated, but the effect is clear: Sabbath violation severs the person from the covenant community.

The phrase *Shabbat shabbaton* (שַׁבַּ֤ת שַׁבָּתוֹן֙, "a Sabbath of complete rest") is a superlative construction — a Sabbath of Sabbaths, the most restful rest, cessation raised to its highest power. The LXX renders this as *sabbata anapausis hagia* (σάββατα ἀνάπαυσις ἁγία, "a holy Sabbath of rest").

**v. 17 — GOD CAUGHT HIS BREATH (Heb. *uvayyom hashevi'i shavat vayyinnafash*, וּבַיּוֹם֙ הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י שָׁבַ֖ת וַיִּנָּפַֽשׁ):** The final word of the Sabbath section is extraordinary. *Vayyinnafash* (וַיִּנָּפַשׁ) is a Niphal form derived from *nefesh* (נֶפֶשׁ, "breath, life, soul, self"). It means something like "he was refreshed" or, more viscerally, "he caught his breath" or "he re-souled himself." The anthropomorphism is startling and deliberate: the God who made the heavens and the earth needed — or at least chose — to catch his breath. The Sabbath is grounded not merely in a divine command but in a divine experience. God rested, and in resting, was refreshed. When Israel rests on the seventh day, they are not merely obeying an ordinance; they are imitating the Creator's own rhythm of exertion and restoration. The LXX tones this down with *katepausen* (κατέπαυσεν, "he ceased, he rested"), losing the bodily force of the Hebrew *vayyinnafash*.

**v. 18 — THE TABLETS: WRITTEN BY THE FINGER OF GOD (Heb. *shenei luchot ha'edut luchot even ketuvim be'etsba' Elohim*, שְׁנֵ֛י לֻחֹ֥ת הָעֵדֻ֖ת לֻחֹ֣ת אָ֑בֶן כְּתֻבִ֖ים בְּאֶצְבַּ֥ע אֱלֹהִֽים):** The chapter ends with an image of devastating simplicity after all the elaborate instructions. *Luchot ha'edut* (לֻחֹת הָעֵדֻת, "tablets of the testimony") — *edut* (עֵדוּת) means "testimony, witness, stipulation," indicating that the tablets are not merely a legal code but a witness to the covenant relationship. They are *luchot even* (לֻחֹת אֶבֶן, "tablets of stone") — the most durable writing surface available, implying permanence and gravity. And they are *ketuvim be'etsba' Elohim* (כְּתוּבִים בְּאֶצְבַּע אֱלֹהִים, "written by the finger of God"). The *etsba'* (אֶצְבַּע, "finger") of God is the same "finger" that worked the plagues in Egypt (Exod. 8:15[19]) and that will write on the wall in Daniel 5:5. It is an anthropomorphism that insists on the directness of divine authorship — no intermediary, no dictation to a scribe. God wrote these words personally, pressing them into stone with the divine hand. The phrase *kekhaloto ledabber itto* (כְּכַלֹּת֗וֹ לְדַבֵּ֤ר אִתּוֹ֙, "when he finished speaking with him") uses *dabber itto* (דַּבֵּר אִתּוֹ, "speaking with him") — the preposition *et/itt* (אִתּ, "with") implies conversation, not monologue. God was speaking *with* Moses, not merely *to* him. The LXX renders this as *epeduken auto* (ἐπέδωκεν αὐτῷ, "he gave to him") for the act of giving, and *gegrammenas to daktulo tou theou* (γεγραμμένας τῷ δακτύλῳ τοῦ θεοῦ, "written by the finger of God") preserving the striking anthropomorphism.

The verse also functions as a narrative hinge. Moses has been on the mountain receiving instructions — for the tabernacle (chs. 25–27), for the priesthood (chs. 28–29), for the altar of incense, the census tax, the basin, the anointing oil, and the incense (ch. 30), for the artisans and the Sabbath (ch. 31) — and now, at last, something tangible is placed in his hands. Stone tablets. The conversation is over; the evidence is in hand. And the reader knows what Moses does not yet know: while he has been receiving these tablets on the mountain, his people at the foot of the mountain have been making a golden calf. The calm of verse 18 is the silence before the storm of chapter 32.

---

## Translator's Reflection

**The artist, the rest, and the finger — when the Spirit's first work was beauty:**

Exodus 31 is a chapter of three movements, each one quietly astonishing. An artist is filled with God's Spirit. A day of rest is declared holier than the work of building God's own house. And two stone tablets are placed in a man's hands, written by a finger that made the stars. Each movement redefines something the reader thought they already understood.

**1. The Spirit's first filling is for art, not for war or prophecy.** This detail should stop every reader in their tracks. The first person in Scripture explicitly described as "filled with the Spirit of God" is not a general leading an army, not a prophet declaring judgment, not a king ruling a nation. It is Bezalel, a craftsman — a man who works with gold, silver, bronze, gemstones, and wood. The Spirit of God, before it empowers conquest or inspires oracle, empowers artistry. The three gifts given to Bezalel — wisdom, understanding, and knowledge — are the same attributes by which God made the world (Prov. 3:19–20). The theological implication is staggering: when a human being creates something beautiful and skillful, they are doing in miniature what God did in Genesis 1. The Spirit that hovered over the waters now hovers over the workbench. The breath that spoke galaxies into being now guides a chisel against stone. Human craftsmanship, at its highest, is a participation in divine creativity. This is not an ancillary detail of the tabernacle narrative; it is one of its central theological claims. The place where God will dwell must be made beautifully, and the beauty must come from the same Spirit that made the world beautiful in the first place.

**2. The Sabbath interrupts the building — and that interruption is the point.** The placement of the Sabbath command here, between the commissioning of the artisans (vv. 1–11) and the giving of the tablets (v. 18), is not accidental. The rabbis noticed it and drew the conclusion: even the construction of the tabernacle — the most sacred building project in Israel's history, commanded by God himself — must stop for the Sabbath. The building of God's house does not override the rhythm of God's rest. This means something profound about the nature of holiness. Holiness is not produced by relentless effort. Holiness includes cessation. The Sabbath reminds Israel that they are not the source of their own sanctification — *ki ani YHWH meqaddishkhem* ("because I am YHWH who makes you holy"). The Sabbath is a weekly confession that human labor, however sacred, is not the engine of redemption. God sanctifies. Humans rest. And in the resting, they learn who actually makes things holy.

The word *vayyinnafash* — "he caught his breath," "he re-souled himself" — is one of the most remarkable words in the Hebrew Bible. After making the universe, God needed to exhale. Not because God is finite, but because rest is woven into the fabric of reality at the deepest level. When God stopped, God was not merely modeling behavior for future humans. God was revealing something about the nature of existence itself: that the rhythm of work and rest is not a concession to human weakness but a reflection of divine character. The universe runs on a pulse — exertion, cessation, exertion, cessation — and that pulse originates in God's own being.

**3. The finger of God closes the conversation and opens the crisis.** Verse 18 is one of the most perfectly placed verses in all of narrative literature. After six chapters of detailed instructions (25–30) and the commissioning of those who will carry them out (31:1–17), God simply hands Moses two stone tablets. The conversation is over. The evidence is tangible. And the reader, who has been immersed in specifications for curtains and clasps and anointing oil and incense, suddenly holds their breath — because the reader knows what is about to happen next.

The phrase "written by the finger of God" insists on something no metaphor should be allowed to soften: God personally inscribed these words. Not through a scribe. Not through dictation. The divine finger pressed into stone and left marks. The God who spoke the world into existence also writes — and writes on the most permanent medium available, stone that does not decay. The permanence of the medium makes the coming destruction of the tablets all the more devastating. What God wrote in stone, Moses will shatter on rock. What the divine finger inscribed, human sin will undo. But we are not there yet. For this one verse, the tablets are intact, the covenant is whole, and God's handwriting is legible. Verse 18 is the last moment of calm in the book of Exodus. Everything that follows — the calf, the breaking, the intercession, the second tablets — flows from the shattering of what this verse so quietly, so carefully, places into Moses' hands.

**4. The chapter weaves together Spirit, rest, and word into a single theology of presence.** Spirit fills the artisan. Rest sanctifies the people. Stone tablets bear the covenant. Together, these three movements answer a single question: how does the infinite God dwell among finite people? Through beauty (the Spirit-filled craftsmanship that builds the dwelling), through rhythm (the Sabbath that teaches Israel they are not the source of their own holiness), and through word (the tablets that encode the terms of the relationship). Art, rest, and word — these are the three pillars of divine habitation. The tabernacle is not merely a building project; it is an ecology of presence, and Exodus 31 names its three essential components.

And all of it — the Spirit in the artisan, the rest in the calendar, the writing on the stone — is about to collide with a golden calf. The artisan's skill will find its dark counterpart in Aaron's improvised casting. The Sabbath's holiness will be mocked by a festival of human invention. The finger-written tablets will shatter at the foot of the very mountain where they were given. Exodus 31 is the deep breath before the plunge. It is the last chapter in which everything is still possible, the final moment before the covenant is broken and must, painfully, be remade.

— *PNEUMA*
